nativeRange.habitat: Temporary shallow pools and flooded grassland depressions on the Pampas; pools disappear in the dry season, leaving eggs dormant in the substrate

decorPreferences: peat or fine dark sand substrate at least 5 cm deep for spawning
dim lighting or floating plants to reduce glare
sparse vegetation (Java moss, hornwort) as cover

diet.notes: Micro-predator; feeds predominantly on small live or frozen invertebrates — live daphnia, baby brine shrimp, micro-worms, grindal worms, and cyclops are ideal. Will often accept frozen bloodworms and cyclops. Dry foods are usually ignored or refused; conditioning pairs for spawning on live foods is strongly recommended.

goodWith: small peaceful characins tolerant of cool water
other annual killifish of similar size kept in separate tanks
avoidWith: small invertebrates (shrimp, snails — will be eaten)
multiple males in small tanks (high conspecific aggression)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Austrolebias nigripinnis (SpecCode 10951); shows male coloration.

summary: A jewel of the Argentine Pampas, the Black Pearlfish is a annual killifish with a lifespan of roughly one year. Males are strikingly iridescent blue-green with black fins; females are drab brown. Eggs survive months of drought buried in peat, hatching when rains return — a diapause life strategy unique to annual killifishes.
careNotes:
Austrolebias nigripinnis is a South American annual killifish from the seasonal pools of the Argentine and Uruguayan Pampas. Its entire wild life cycle is compressed into the pool’s wet season: fish hatch, mature, spawn, and die within roughly 10–12 months, leaving behind drought-resistant eggs buried in the mud substrate that re-hatch when rains return the following year.

In the aquarium, a species tank of at least 40 L is recommended for a single pair or small group. Temperature should be kept in the cool subtropical range of 16–22°C; temperatures above 24°C accelerate aging and shorten lifespan. The species is well-suited to unheated or lightly heated setups in temperate homes. Soft, acidic to neutral water (pH 6.0–7.0, gH 2–12) mirrors the soft rainwater that fills Pampas pools. A tight-fitting lid is essential as the species is an active jumper.

Substrate is critical: a peat or fine dark sand bed of at least 5 cm depth allows the pair to spawn by diving into the substrate and depositing eggs. Without adequate spawning medium, reproductive success drops sharply. A separate shallow container of moist peat can be placed in the tank as a dedicated spawning site and then removed for dry storage at room temperature for 8–12 weeks to simulate the dry season before re-wetting to trigger hatching.

Males are territorial and will compete aggressively with one another; keep only one male per tank unless the aquarium is large enough to establish distinct territories. A ratio of one male to one or two females is typical. Males are conspicuously colored — iridescent blue-green with dark fin borders — while females are cryptically brown, consistent with their role as egg-guarders in shallow cover.

Feeding primarily on live or frozen micro-invertebrates (baby brine shrimp, daphnia, micro-worms, grindal worms), A. nigripinnis rarely accepts dry foods. The short lifespan means consistent live-food conditioning is important throughout the fish’s life to maintain peak condition and reproductive output.
breedingNotes:
Breeding Austrolebias nigripinnis requires a peat or soft-sand substrate of at least 5 cm depth. The pair spawns by diving side-by-side into the substrate and depositing eggs, a behavior called bottom-spawning or substrate-spawning, characteristic of annual killifishes in the subfamily Cynolebiinae. Spawning activity is most intense in the first few months of life.

After a spawning period (typically 4–6 weeks of regular observation), remove the peat containing eggs, press out excess water, and store loosely moist in a sealed plastic bag or container at 20–22°C for 8–12 weeks to simulate the dry-season diapause. Eggs are visible as small amber spheres in the peat. After the diapause period, add soft, slightly acidic water to the peat to trigger hatching; fry usually emerge within 24–48 hours.

Fry are relatively large and will accept newly hatched brine shrimp (nauplii) and micro-worms from day one. They grow quickly and reach sexual maturity within 6–8 weeks. Because the species is annual, it is important to cycle new generations regularly — the parent fish will not survive the full year in most aquarium setups. Breeding difficulty is rated moderate due to the need for proper peat diapause management rather than any difficulty in conditioning the adults to spawn.

primary.name: FishBase — Austrolebias nigripinnis (SpecCode 10951)
primary.notes: Primary source. Confirmed max size 7.0 cm TL (male/unsexed), common length 4.5 cm; temperature 18–22°C; pH 6.0–7.0; dH 5–12; distribution Lower Paraná and Uruguay River basins, South America; diet worms, crustaceans and insects; bottom-spawning with 2–4 month incubation; max reported age 1 year; IUCN Near Threatened (B2b(i,ii,iii), 2021); family Rivulidae, order Cyprinodontiformes.

additional.name: Seriously Fish — Austrolebias species genus page
additional.notes: SeriouslyFish does not host a dedicated A. nigripinnis profile page; genus-level page confirmed but species-specific page returned 404. Data derived from FishBase and established killifish literature.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Killifish species overview
additional.notes: Aquarium Co-Op does not host a dedicated annual killifish or Austrolebias care page. General killifish care norms (live food preference, peat spawning, cool water tolerance) are well-established in the killifish hobby and corroborated by FishBase ecology data.

additional.name: IUCN Red List — Austrolebias nigripinnis
additional.notes: IUCN Near Threatened status (assessed 2021) cited from FishBase ecology section; reflects ongoing habitat loss from Pampas grassland drainage and agriculture affecting seasonal pool ecosystems.

fish.breedingNotes: Bottom-spawner into peat or soft sand; eggs require 8–12 weeks dry diapause to hatch. Fry accept nauplii from day one. Moderate difficulty due to peat management requirements, not adult conditioning.
